query: What are CSS custom properties and how are they used in production?

competitors.label: Using CSS custom properties (variables)
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN guide for CSS custom properties, including declaration, var() usage, scope, inheritance, fallbacks, and runtime behavior.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on custom property syntax, inheritance, fallbacks, and runtime behavior.
theirGaps: The guide is less interview-shaped and less compact about the specific production distinction between runtime browser variables and build-time preprocessor variables.

realWorldUseCases:
Overview

CSS custom properties are variables defined with the -- prefix and accessed using the var() function. They differ from preprocessor variables (like SASS or LESS) because they’re live in the browser — inheritable, changeable with JavaScript, and scoped within selectors.
followUpConfusion:
Fallback Values
Use fallback values when a variable might not exist:
color: var(--heading-color, #333);

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains custom properties thoroughly, but our page is more explicit about why teams actually adopt them: runtime theming, scoped overrides, and browser-level values instead of build-time substitution.
actionableExamples: Its examples for declaration, var(), and fallback usage are strong, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the likely follow-up: CSS custom properties are not Sass variables, and inheritance plus fallback behavior is exactly what makes them useful in design systems.

actionableExamples: Keep the :root example and runtime-update example visible so the answer stays faster to scan than a long reference guide.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical custom-properties guide, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it packages syntax, scope, inheritance, fallback, and runtime theming into one production-focused mental model.

competitors.label: CSS Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's CSS interview bank is directly relevant because variables, theming, and selector-level styling conventions are common CSS interview topics.
theirStrengths: It is a strong CSS interview-prep hub with broad coverage of modern CSS concepts and design-system-adjacent topics.
theirGaps: The category page is not dedicated to custom properties, so it is less focused on inheritance, fallbacks, and runtime theme toggling.

realWorldUseCases:
Practical scenario
Implement light/dark themes by switching CSS variables on :root and letting components react automatically.

Common pitfalls
  • Missing fallbacks when variables are undefined.
  • Confusing CSS variables (runtime) with preprocessor variables (build-time).
  • Scoping variables too narrowly so children cannot inherit them.
Trade-off or test tip
Variables allow runtime theming but require careful defaults. Test by toggling themes and verifying contrast and inheritance.
followUpConfusion:
Scoping & Inheritance
Variables defined in :root are global. They can be overridden locally inside specific components or containers, enabling theming or context-aware customization.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant for CSS prep, but the hub page is less explicit about the exact runtime theming and inheritance questions that make custom properties useful in production.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong interview bank, so the worked-example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the likely follow-up confusion: scope, inheritance, and fallback behavior are features, not implementation accidents.

actionableExamples: Keep the dark-theme override example near the top so the answer stays interview-ready.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad CSS prep, but our page is more specific about the exact scope, inheritance, and runtime-theme behavior that interviewers usually want explained.

competitors.label: Custom properties
competitors.selectionReason: This is a strong topic-direct tutorial comparator for CSS variables because it focuses on reuse, var(), fallbacks, root scope, and override patterns in modern CSS.
theirStrengths: web.dev is strong on practical custom-property usage, including organization, fallback values, and cascade-driven overrides.
theirGaps: It is less compact as an interview answer and less explicit about the exact runtime-versus-build-time distinction that often causes confusion.

realWorldUseCases:
Dynamic Updates with JavaScript
Unlike preprocessor variables, custom properties exist in the DOM and can be modified at runtime.
followUpConfusion:
Best Practices
  • Define global tokens in :root for theme consistency.
  • Use local overrides for component variants or dark/light modes.
  • Combine with media queries for responsive design (@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)).
  • Maintain a naming convention like --color-primary, --spacing-md.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is strong on practical CSS variable usage, but our page is more focused on the production decision points: when to scope locally, when to keep values global, and how to test theme toggles safely.
actionableExamples: Its custom-property examples are strong, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the follow-up teams usually hit first: custom properties inherit, can be changed at runtime, and need fallbacks and sensible scope boundaries.

actionableExamples: Keep the runtime update and root override examples visible so the answer stays more operational than a broader tutorial.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is a strong custom-properties tutorial, but our page is more useful as an interview answer because it ties syntax, inheritance, fallbacks, and runtime theming into one production-oriented explanation.
